explanation:
Generative AI creates new content — text, images, code, music — by learning patterns from training data. Unlike discriminative models that classify inputs ("is this a cat?"), generative models learn the underlying distribution of data and sample from it to produce novel outputs. It's the difference between an art critic (discriminative) and an artist (generative).

The key breakthrough was the Transformer architecture (2017), which enabled models to understand long-range dependencies in sequences. Combined with massive datasets and compute, this produced Large Language Models (GPT, Claude, Gemini) for text, Diffusion Models (DALL-E, Stable Diffusion) for images, and specialized models for code, audio, and video.
example:
```python
# Understanding generative vs discriminative models

# Discriminative: classifies existing data
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import numpy as np

X_train = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6], [7, 8]])
y_train = np.array([0, 0, 1, 1])  # Two classes

clf = RandomForestClassifier()
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
print("Discriminative prediction:", clf.predict([[4, 5]]))  # Class 0 or 1

# Generative: creates new data points
# Simple example using a Gaussian Mixture Model
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture

gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=2)
gmm.fit(X_train)

# Generate NEW data points that follow the same distribution
new_samples, _ = gmm.sample(5)
print("Generated samples:\n", new_samples)
# These are NEW points that look like the training data

# The concept at scale:
# - GPT generates text by predicting next tokens
# - DALL-E generates images from text descriptions
# - Codex generates code from natural language
# All learn the distribution of their training data
```
key_takeaway: Generative AI learns the distribution of training data to create new, original content — unlike discriminative models that only classify existing inputs.
try_it: Train a GaussianMixture on a dataset of your choice (e.g., iris dataset features), generate 10 new synthetic samples, and compare their distribution to the original data.

explanation:
The Transformer is the engine behind modern generative AI. Before Transformers, models processed sequences one token at a time (RNNs, LSTMs) — slow and forgetful of distant context. Transformers process all tokens simultaneously using self-attention, which lets each token "attend to" every other token to understand relationships regardless of distance.

Self-attention computes three vectors per token: Query (what am I looking for?), Key (what do I contain?), and Value (what information do I share?). The dot product of Query and Key determines how much attention to pay, and the weighted sum of Values produces the output. Multi-head attention runs this process in parallel with different learned projections, capturing diverse relationship types.
example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class SelfAttention(nn.Module):
    """Simplified self-attention mechanism."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim: int, num_heads: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads

        self.W_q = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim = x.shape

        # Compute Q, K, V
        Q = self.W_q(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.W_k(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.W_v(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        # Attention scores: how much each token attends to others
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.head_dim)
        attention_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)

        # Weighted sum of values
        output = torch.matmul(attention_weights, V)
        output = output.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim)
        return self.W_o(output)

# Demo
embed_dim = 64
num_heads = 4
seq_len = 10
batch_size = 2

attention = SelfAttention(embed_dim, num_heads)
tokens = torch.randn(batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim)  # Batch of token embeddings

output = attention(tokens)
print(f"Input shape: {tokens.shape}")
print(f"Output shape: {output.shape}")
print(f"Each token now has context from all {seq_len} positions")
```
key_takeaway: Self-attention lets every token attend to every other token simultaneously — Q×K determines importance, weighted V produces context-aware representations.
try_it: Modify the attention to include a causal mask (upper triangular) so tokens can only attend to previous positions. This is how autoregressive models like GPT work.

explanation:
GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) generates text by predicting the next token given all previous tokens. During training, it reads billions of text examples and learns the probability distribution P(next_token | previous_tokens). During inference, it samples from this distribution one token at a time, building output autoregressively.

Sampling strategies control output quality: greedy decoding (always pick the most likely token) is deterministic but repetitive; temperature (0-2) controls randomness (low = conservative, high = creative); top-p (nucleus sampling) limits choices to the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds p; top-k limits to the k most likely tokens.
example:
```python
import openai
import json

client = openai.OpenAI()

prompt = "The future of software development is"

# Compare sampling strategies
def generate(temperature, top_p=1.0, max_tokens=50):
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        temperature=temperature,
        top_p=top_p,
        max_tokens=max_tokens
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

# Greedy (temperature=0): deterministic, safe
print("Greedy (t=0):", generate(temperature=0))

# Balanced (temperature=0.7): coherent with variety
print("\nBalanced (t=0.7):", generate(temperature=0.7))

# Creative (temperature=1.5): diverse, less predictable
print("\nCreative (t=1.5):", generate(temperature=1.5))

# Nucleus sampling: top 90% of probability mass
print("\nNucleus (p=0.9):", generate(temperature=0.8, top_p=0.9))

# Token-by-token generation to see probabilities
def generate_with_logprobs():
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Python is a"}],
        max_tokens=5,
        logprobs=True,
        top_logprobs=3  # Show top 3 candidates per position
    )

    choice = response.choices[0]
    for token_info in choice.logprobs.content:
        print(f"\nPosition: chose '{token_info.token}'")
        for candidate in token_info.top_logprobs:
            print(f"  '{candidate.token}': {candidate.logprob:.3f} ({candidate.logprob * 100 / 2.303:.1f}%)")

generate_with_logprogs()
```
key_takeaway: GPT generates text token-by-token using temperature (randomness), top-p (nucleus sampling), and top-k to control the balance between creativity and coherence.
try_it: Generate the same prompt with temperatures 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5. Compare the outputs — at what temperature does the text become incoherent?

explanation:
Diffusion models generate images by learning to reverse a noise process. During training, they progressively add Gaussian noise to images until only noise remains, then learn to reverse each step. At inference, they start from pure noise and denoise step by step, gradually revealing a coherent image. It's like a sculptor removing marble to reveal a statue.

The key components are: a U-Net (neural network that predicts noise at each step), a text encoder (CLIP converts prompts to embeddings), and a scheduler (controls the denoising steps). Text conditioning guides the denoising process so the generated image matches the prompt. Classifier-free guidance strengthens prompt adherence at the cost of diversity.
example:
```python
from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline, DPMSolverMultistepScheduler
import torch

# Load a diffusion model
model_id = "stabilityai/stable-diffusion-2-1"
pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    safety_checker=None  # Disable for demo
)
pipe = pipe.to("cuda")

# Use a faster scheduler
pipe.scheduler = DPMSolverMultistepScheduler.from_config(pipe.scheduler.config)

# Generate an image
prompt = "A serene Japanese garden with cherry blossoms, watercolor style"
negative_prompt = "blurry, low quality, distorted"

image = pipe(
    prompt=prompt,
    negative_prompt=negative_prompt,
    num_inference_steps=25,      # More steps = higher quality
    guidance_scale=7.5,          # Higher = stronger prompt adherence
    width=512,
    height=512,
    generator=torch.Generator("cuda").manual_seed(42)  # Reproducible
).images[0]

image.save("generated_garden.png")
print(f"Image saved: {image.size}")

# Understanding the pipeline internals
def explain_diffusion_steps():
    """Show what happens at each denoising step."""
    from diffusers import DDIMScheduler
    import numpy as np

    scheduler = DDIMScheduler.from_pretrained(model_id, subfolder="scheduler")
    scheduler.set_timesteps(10)  # Fewer steps for visualization

    print("Denoising timesteps:", scheduler.timesteps)
    # Timesteps go from high noise (1000) to low noise (0)
    # Each step removes a predicted amount of noise
    for i, t in enumerate(scheduler.timesteps):
        print(f"Step {i}: timestep={t}, sigma={scheduler.sigmas[i]:.4f}")

explain_diffusion_steps()
```
key_takeaway: Diffusion models generate images by iteratively denoising random noise — text prompts guide the process via CLIP embeddings, and guidance scale controls prompt adherence.
try_it: Generate the same prompt with guidance_scale values of 3, 7.5, and 15. How does increasing guidance affect image quality and diversity?

explanation:
Code generation models (Codex, CodeLlama, StarCoder) are trained on billions of lines of code from GitHub, Stack Overflow, and documentation. They understand programming syntax, common patterns, and can translate between languages. Unlike general LLMs, code models are optimized for producing syntactically valid, functional code.

Effective code generation requires clear specifications: describe what the function should do, specify input/output types, provide example usage, and state constraints. The more context you give (existing code, error messages, framework version), the better the generated code fits your project.
key_takeaway: Code generation works best with clear specs (types, examples, constraints), low temperature for deterministic output, and context about your project's framework and patterns.
try_it: Write a prompt that generates a complete FastAPI endpoint with Pydantic models, error handling, and unit tests from a natural language description.

explanation:
Prompting adapts a pre-trained model to your task by providing instructions and examples at inference time. Fine-tuning adapts the model itself by continuing training on your specific data. Think of prompting as giving a skilled employee a detailed memo, while fine-tuning is sending them to specialized training.

When to prompt: task is well-defined with clear instructions, you need flexibility to change behavior, data is limited (<100 examples), or you want to use the latest model. When to fine-tune: you need consistent formatting, domain-specific vocabulary, reduced latency (shorter prompts), or the base model consistently fails at your task despite good prompts.
example:
```python
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()

# Prompting approach: few-shot examples
system_prompt = """You are a medical report summarizer.
Convert clinical notes into patient-friendly summaries.

Format: {"summary": str, "key_actions": [str], "follow_up": str}

Example input: "Pt presents with persistent cough x3 weeks, low-grade temp 99.8F. CXR shows RLL infiltrate. Started on azithromycin 500mg."

Example output: {"summary": "You have a chest infection in your right lung that's been causing a cough and mild fever for 3 weeks.", "key_actions": ["Started antibiotic (azithromycin)"], "follow_up": "Return if symptoms worsen or don't improve in 5 days"}

Now convert the following:"""

prompt_result = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Pt with hx of T2DM presents with polyuria, polydipsia. A1c 9.2%. Increased metformin to 1000mg BID. Referral to endocrinology."}
    ],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"}
)
print("Prompt-based:", prompt_result.choices[0].message.content)

# Fine-tuning approach: prepare training data
import json

training_examples = [
    {
        "messages": [
            {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
            {"role": "user", "content": "Clinical note here..."},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": json.dumps({"summary": "Patient friendly version", "key_actions": ["action1"], "follow_up": "follow up plan"})}
        ]
    },
    # ... hundreds more examples
]

# Save as JSONL for OpenAI fine-tuning
with open("training_data.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for example in training_examples:
        f.write(json.dumps(example) + "\n")

# Upload and create fine-tuning job
# file = client.files.create(file=open("training_data.jsonl", "rb"), purpose="fine-tune")
# job = client.fine_tuning.jobs.create(training_file=file.id, model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18")

# Decision framework
"""
Use PROMPTING when:
- Task changes frequently
- You have <100 examples
- You need the latest model capabilities
- You want zero training cost

Use FINE-TUNING when:
- You need consistent output format
- Domain has specialized vocabulary
- Base model fails despite good prompts
- You need shorter prompts (lower latency/cost)
"""
```
key_takeaway: Prompting is flexible and zero-cost; fine-tuning is for consistent formatting and domain expertise. Always try prompting first — fine-tune only when prompts consistently fail.
try_it: Create a few-shot prompt for classifying support tickets (bug, feature request, question). Test it on 5 examples. Would fine-tuning improve consistency?

explanation:
Generative AI inherits biases from training data, can generate harmful content, and may produce convincing misinformation. Responsible AI means understanding these risks and implementing safeguards: content filtering, bias auditing, transparency about AI-generated content, and human oversight for high-stakes decisions.

Key concerns include: bias (models may reflect societal prejudices in training data), hallucination (confident but false statements), privacy (models may memorize training data), and misuse (generating spam, deepfakes, or malicious code). Mitigation strategies include red-teaming, output filtering, watermarking, and clear disclosure policies.
key_takeaway: Responsible AI requires multi-layer safety: input moderation, output filtering, bias auditing, PII detection, and transparent disclosure that content is AI-generated.
try_it: Add a bias detection layer to `ContentModerator` that flags responses containing gendered or racial assumptions by checking for stereotypical associations.

explanation:
Generative AI problems test your understanding of how models create new content — text, images, code — and how to control their output. The core patterns are: prompt engineering (controlling generation), sampling strategies (temperature, top-p, top-k), fine-tuning (adapting pre-trained models), and safety (content moderation, bias detection). Understanding the difference between discriminative and generative models is foundational.

Interview questions focus on implementing generation algorithms from scratch (sampling, beam search), understanding transformer internals (attention, positional encoding), and building production pipelines (RAG, guardrails, evaluation). The field moves fast, so demonstrating knowledge of current best practices matters.
key_takeaway: Generative AI is about controlling output quality through sampling strategies and prompt engineering. Temperature controls randomness, top-p/nucleus limits choices, and fine-tuning adapts to specific domains.
try_it: Implement beam search for text generation and compare its output quality against greedy and random sampling.

practice_challenges.title: Implement Temperature Sampling
practice_challenges.hint: Divide logits by temperature before softmax. Low temp = deterministic, high temp = random.
practice_challenges.description: Implement a sampling function that takes logits and a temperature parameter, applies temperature scaling, and samples the next token. Compare outputs at temperatures 0.1, 0.7, and 1.5.
practice_challenges.expected_output: Sampled tokens at different temperatures showing the effect on diversity

practice_challenges.title: Build a Content Moderation System
practice_challenges.hint: Layer pattern matching with an LLM-based classifier
practice_challenges.description: Build a multi-layer content moderation system that checks user inputs with regex patterns, then uses an LLM to classify flagged content. Track false positive/negative rates.
practice_challenges.expected_output: Moderation decisions with flagged categories and confidence scores

practice_challenges.title: Implement Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling
practice_challenges.hint: Sort by probability descending, accumulate until reaching p, then renormalize
practice_challenges.description: Implement nucleus sampling that sorts tokens by probability, keeps the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds p, and renormalizes. Compare with top-k sampling.
practice_challenges.expected_output: Filtered probability distributions and sampled tokens for both methods

practice_challenges.title: Build a RAG Pipeline from Scratch
practice_challenges.hint: Chunk documents, embed chunks, search by cosine similarity, inject into prompt
practice_challenges.description: Build a complete RAG pipeline: chunk a document, embed chunks, create a vector store, retrieve relevant chunks for a query, and generate an answer grounded in the retrieved context.
practice_challenges.expected_output: Answers with source citations showing which chunks were used

practice_challenges.title: Implement LoRA Fine-tuning
practice_challenges.hint: Freeze base weights, add low-rank matrices A and B where delta_W = B*A
practice_challenges.description: Implement LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) for a transformer model. Freeze base weights, add trainable low-rank adapters, and fine-tune on a small dataset. Compare trainable parameter count with full fine-tuning.
practice_challenges.expected_output: Training loss curve and comparison of trainable vs total parameters
